summary: Uncle Bob examines the persistent gender gap in programming, noting that despite high demand and growing salaries, women represent only about 5% of developers even though they earn 65% of college degrees. He traces potential 'shunts' in the pipeline from childhood play preferences through high school, college, and careers. After dismissing hiring discrimination and college bias as unlikely causes given market pressures, he argues that quota programs would be counterproductive—they wouldn't increase overall female employment (demand is already high) and would create environments where women are systematically outperformed by male colleagues who faced higher hiring standards.
key_points: The programmer shortage is acute (1 hire per 3 job postings) yet the industry effectively ignores half the population, with women representing only ~6% of developers
The 'pipeline' has potential shunts at every stage—from childhood play preferences favoring boys' engagement with computers, through the 'high school trap,' to career attrition after graduation
Market forces make active hiring discrimination unlikely: companies starved for talent can't afford to filter on anything but merit

Quota programs would not increase total female employment (demand already exceeds supply) but would redirect top female talent to quota-setting companies while lowering average female performance relative to male colleagues everywhere
The real solution lies in upstream interventions: encouraging girls' interest in programming through education and outreach programs starting in childhood
money_quotes: So five times as many men than women graduate with BSCS degrees. Then, nearly two out of three of those women do not pursue a programming career.
Businesses that are starved for programmers can't afford to filter based on gender. They can't afford to filter based on anything except merit, and they'll have to compete for that.
If a plurality women students expressed interest in computer science, the universities would bend over backwards to accommodate them.
There seems to me to be little downside in encouraging young girls and young women to consider programming as an entertaining activity, something fun to learn, and eventually a good career.
No more women get hired overall, because demand is too high to allow any candidate, male or female, to go unhired.
What quotas will do, however, is to redirect women into those companies that employ quotas, and redirect men into those companies that do not.
They must also face the ethical question of bypassing more qualified candidates in favor of gender – which is clearly discriminatory.
key_insight: The programming gender gap stems from pipeline problems beginning in early childhood, not hiring discrimination, and quota programs would paradoxically worsen women's relative position by creating systematic performance disparities while doing nothing to increase overall female employment.
